Fern Por-rrrcAr- PnecrrcEs CoMMrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95811-2329
\916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
May 20, 2008
Richard Booth
35 Martha Road
Orinda, CA94563
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-072
Dear Mr. Booth:
'l'his letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest
prt;visitltts tlf the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), and is based on the facts presented in
vo(lr reqllL'st for advice. Because your question is general in nature, \4/e are treating your
rcquest as one fbr informal assistance.r
QUESTTONS
Would you have a conflict of interest if you were to receive a referral fee from the
dcveloper of a certain housing project who will seek Planning Commission approval of
lot designs lbr this project?
CONCLUSION
Nothing in the Act prohibits you from accepting a referral tee fronr the tleveloper.
If, however, you receive (or are promised) such a fee or fees totaling $500 or more during
the 12 months prior to the decision(s) you describe, it appears likell, that you would have
a conflict of itlterest in that decision, and wor.rld be required to recuse yourself from that
decision as described below.
I Infornral assistance does not provide the request()r with the immunity providc'd by an opini11n or
firrmal written advice. (section tt3 I l,l: Regulation lgi29(cX-l). copy enclosecl.)


File No. l-08-072
Page No.2
FACTS
You are a licensed real estate broker primarily selling residential real estate, and
have been a Planning Commissioner in the City of Orinda for about one year. There is a
housing development within Orinda that has been in the development stage for many
years. Prior to your appointment to the Planning Commission the City approved this
subdivision and entitled the land. The developer is now in the process of obtaining
design review approvals from the Planning Commission for home designs which will be
offered as "possible" designs to buyers who purchase lots within this subdivision. The
developer will not be building any of the homes.
The Planning Commission has thus far approved four home designs for four
specific lots, and another five designs have been submitted for another five specific lots.
The developer expects to present a total of 18 different designs, and then to request that
the Planning Commission approve a distribution of those approved designs across the 245
lots. You have participated in all of the design review discussions and decisions to date.
The developer recently announced that it is offering a three percent referral fee to
any licensed real estate agent who refers a buyer who purchases a lot through the
developer's own sales force. You ask about the potential for conflicts of interest to arise
should you receive one or more of these referral fees in your private capacity, while you
continue to serve on the planning Commission.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100
The Act does not prevent a public official from receiving income in compensation
for work performed in the private sector while holding office. Section 87100 prohibits a
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he or she has a financial interest.
A public official's private employment may at times cause the official to have a conflict
of interest in certain governmental decisions.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framework for deciding
whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The
general ntle, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes
a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her economic interests.


File No. l-08-072
Page No.3
Step l: Are you a public official?
Under the Act, a public official is "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) As a member of the planning
Commission, you are considered to be a "public official."
Step 2: Are you making, participating in, or influencing a governmental decision?
If as a member of the Planning Commission you will be called upon to consider
the approval of one or more of the developer's proposed designs, or the distribution of
designs across the development's 245 lots, you will be making or participating in making
a governmental decision. (section 87 100; Regulations 1 g702-1 g702.4.)
Step 3: Do you have a potentially disqualifying economic interest in the decision?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain specific economic interests, described in Section 87103 and Regulations
18703-18703.5. A public official has an economic interest:
o In a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a).)
o In a business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section g7103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. l(b).)
o In real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
o In any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section g7103(c); Rigulation
18703.3.)
In any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts roral $390 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regularion 19703.4.)
In his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those
of his or her immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the ..personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 19703.5.)


File No. I-08-072
Page No. 4
The only economic interest you have disclosed to us is a possible economic
interest in the developer, which might become a "source of income" to you.l If it paid or
promised to pay you a referral fee totaling $500 or more within 12 monihs prior to the
decision, the developer would then become a potentially disqualifying economic interest.
A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) A govemmental decision
will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will "uur. 
personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate famity to increase or
decrease. (lbid.) You have not suggested that the decision(s) in question here could have
an-effect on your personal finances, and we therefore limit our discussion to possible
effects on the developer, if and when the developer becomes a source of income to you
after you make a successful referral.
Step 4: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly affected by the decisions?
Regulation 18704.1(aXl) provides that a person that is a source of income to an
official is directly involved in a govemmental deiision before the official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding in which tlie decision
will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, oisimilar re-quest, or is a named
party in or is the subject of the proceeding. It appears therefore that the developer will be
directly involved in the Planning Commission decision(s) you have described regarding
approval of the developer's designs.
Step 5: will the financial effect on the economic interest be material?
A conflict of interest may exist only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interestsls material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Regulation 13704.1(b) provides that the materiality standard for a source of
income directly involved in a governmental decision is set by Regulation 1g705.(3(a):
(a) Directly involved sources of income. Any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is u ,our"" tf
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in
a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material.
Step 6: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the economic interest will be materially
affected by the decisions?
An effect upon an economic interest is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regularion 18706ia).) A financial
2 we presume that you also have other economic interests - fbr example in your real estate
business and other clients - but you have not asked us to consider any other econ,rmic interests, so we
confine ourselves to the question you have asked.


File No. I-08-072
Page No. 5
effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. rqs). The determination of
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable mlteriality standard will be met
for your economic interests is necessarity a raciual il;rti". that you mllst answer from
your understanding ol th: facts and by uiing ,o-. ,.uronable and objective mettroo of
valuation. (Hensley Advice Lerter, Nt. e_OZ_f 13: MoockAdvice Letter, No. A_01_140;
o'Horra Advice Letter, No. A-00-r74.) In this case, however, where cuty financiar
effect at all is deemed to be material, it would appear to be reasonably foreseeable that
the decision(s) you describe would have reasonuuty foreseeable material financial effects
on the developer.3
steps 7 and 8: Are there any exceptions to the conflicts of interest rules?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may sometimes
participate in the decision under the "public generally".*."ptiorr. This exception applies
only when the financial effect of a decisio, J, u puuiic oiii"iut,, economic interests is
substantially the same as the effect on a signifi"ur, r"g*"ni of the public generally.
The "legally required participation" rule permits an official to take part in a
governmental decision when the official's participarion in the decisio, i, i"g;; required.
(Section 87101; Regulation 18708.) Your account of the facts does not indicate that
either of these exceptions has any application here.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gl6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General CounselLx
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTwjgl

a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: t I I immJiately prior to the discussion of the
item' identify on the record of the m;ting .o.n type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as detaits of rhe economic interesr. as discussed in'reguraaon i riloi.lJil, (2 ) recuse himself or hersetf ; and
(3) leave the room fbr the duratitln of the discussion ind/or unr. nn rt.-i,em. For closed sessions, consent
calendars' absences and speaking as a member of the pubric ;;;;;l;p;rsonar interests. speciar rures
fbund in regularion 18702.5(c) ora tSZOZ.S(d) appty.

dcveloper of a certain housing project who will seek Planning Commission approval of
lot designs lbr this project?

CONCLUSION: Nothing in the Act prohibits you from accepting a referral tee fronr the tleveloper.
If, however, you receive (or are promised) such a fee or fees totaling $500 or more during
the 12 months prior to the decision(s) you describe, it appears likell, that you would have
a conflict of itlterest in that decision, and wor.rld be required to recuse yourself from that
decision as described below.
